behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit beats Integration because this behavior is the heuristic’s approximation-quality math on in-memory stop sets, with no required module handoff to manifest the failure. Unit then beats System because no real OS/network/subprocess is required. Rejection gate: rejected Integration/System since behavior text is only about route-length ratio for "inputs up to 20 stops," not service wiring or infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Generate deterministic stop sets (n<=20), compute optimal length via exact solver in-process, run heuristic, assert heuristic_length/optimal_length <= 1.5 for each case.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit beats Integration because the failure boundary is the calculation rule (time from distance and road-type speed), which can be exercised as pure function logic with config values as inputs. Unit then beats System because no real infrastructure is needed. Rejection gate: rejected Integration/System since behavior text specifies formula inputs (distance, configured average speed), not cross-module or external dependency behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven tests over (distance, road_type_speed) pairs, asserting per-leg and cumulative time-window outputs match expected arithmetic and rounding rules.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Unit beats Integration because this is a local validation rule on two inputs (estimated total vs remaining shift) plus excess computation. Unit then beats System because no OS/network/subprocess is needed. Rejection gate: rejected Integration/System since behavior text is strictly validator logic: "rejects... returning the excess amount."
behaviors.test_description: Unit tests with boundary values (equal, just under, just over, large overrun) asserting accept/reject decision and exact excess duration returned.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit because the failure appears at module handoff: geocoding result filtering -> optimizer input -> skipped-address report. Integration beats System because behavior text explicitly allows simulated API outcome ("API returns no results") and does not require real HTTP semantics to reproduce the logic failure. Integration brake: (a) no required real OS/network/subprocess; (b) no AI/ML output dependence; (c) no multi-agentic chain; (d) no LLM stage handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Wire service with in-process fake geocoder returning coordinates for N-1 addresses and empty result for one. Assert optimized route excludes failed address, remaining addresses are routed, and response includes exact skipped-address list.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Pairwise: Integration beats Unit because the behavior spans deduplication/aggregation plus downstream routing effect ("not visited twice"), i.e., state handoff across modules. Integration beats System because no real HTTP/OS/subprocess behavior is required to expose this bug. Rejection gate: rejected System since behavior text is in-process data handling of input list and route construction, not infrastructure interaction. Integration brake: no AI/ML dependence and no multi-agentic sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Run service end-to-end in-process with duplicate-address inputs carrying distinct item payloads. Assert output has one stop for that address, items are merged correctly, and final route contains that stop exactly once.
